canonical_definition: Across the provided literature, XStoryCloze is predominantly characterized as a multilingual benchmark designed to evaluate story understanding and commonsense reasoning. The task is consistently operationalized by requiring a model to choose the correct ending for a four-sentence story. Its application for assessing multilingual and cross-lingual capabilities is a recurring theme, with one paper using it to "evaluate multilingual general and cross-lingual generation capabilities" ("LyapLock: Bounded Knowledge Preservation in Sequential Large Language Model Editing"). The benchmark is also frequently used to measure reasoning abilities; some papers explicitly state it measures "Commonsense understanding," while another describes it as a "widely used generic reasoning dataset" ("from Benign import Toxic: Jailbreaking the Language Model via Adversarial Metaphors"). A less common framing, from a single paper, also positions it as a tool for evaluating "multi-turn dialogues" ("CODI: Compressing Chain-of-Thought into Continuous Space via Self-Distillation").
papers: AfroCS-xs: Creating a Compact, High-Quality, Human-Validated Code-Switched Dataset forAfrican Languages
Beyond N-Grams: Rethinking Evaluation Metrics and Strategies for Multilingual Abstractive Summarization
KERL: Knowledge-Enhanced Personalized Recipe Recommendation using Large Language Models
Your Model is Overconfident, and Other Lies We Tell Ourselves

CODI: Compressing Chain-of-Thought into Continuous Space via Self-Distillation
Emergent morpho-phonological representations in self-supervised speech models
Leveraging Text-to-Text Transformers as Classifier Chain for Few-Shot Multi-Label Classification
LyapLock: Bounded Knowledge Preservation in Sequential Large Language Model Editing
